verdict_reasoning: ATG9A is a multi-pass transmembrane protein involved in autophagy. It is canonically localized to the trans-Golgi network (TGN), late endosomes, and recycling endosomes — not the plasma membrane at baseline. However, ATG9A undergoes documented vesicular trafficking cycling between intracellular compartments and the PM during autophagy induction. Upon starvation or autophagy stimulation, ATG9A-positive vesicles traffic to the autophagosome assembly site, and dynamic trafficking to and from the PM is required for autophagosome nucleation. This constitutes a dual_localization / cell_state_induced scenario: the protein has a documented PM pool under autophagy-activated (non-baseline) conditions, mediated by its own multi-pass TM architecture.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Uncertainty over whether ATG9A reaches the outer leaflet of the PM (accessible from outside) vs. trafficking through PM-contact sites (ER-PM junctions, TETHER-mediated contacts) without stable residence on the outer face. Direct surface-proteomics or surface-biotinylation data showing ATG9A accessibility on intact non-permeabilized cells under autophagy induction is not widely cited in the literature; most evidence focuses on intracellular vesicle trafficking and Atg interactions. If PM residence is limited to contact-site juxtaposition without exposure to the outer leaflet, the verdict would shift toward `no`.
